text:
Create a Server Action that creates a new post. The posts list uses cache tags for caching.

After creating the post, invalidate the "posts" cache tag. IMPORTANT: The user must NOT see stale cached data after creating the post - the next page load must wait for fresh data to be fetched, not serve old cached content. Use the appropriate Next.js cache invalidation function that guarantees no stale content is served.
I’m locating the posts flow and the existing cache-tag usage first so I can wire the action into the right server-side path and use the stricter invalidation API, not the softer tag refresh.

text: The repo is tiny; I’ve confirmed the app entry point is just a placeholder. Next I’m reading the page and package metadata so I can implement the action in the simplest compatible Next.js shape.
I’ve got the app shape. I’m verifying the Next.js cache API against the official docs now because the distinction between tag refresh and hard invalidation matters for your stale-data requirement.

I’ve confirmed the stricter API: `updateTag('posts')` is the one that guarantees the next request blocks on fresh data after a server action. I’m checking whether the project has cache components enabled so I can use `cacheTag` cleanly in the list fetcher.

I have enough context to edit now. I’m going to replace the placeholder page with a minimal posts UI, add a Server Action in its own file, and tag the cached posts read so `updateTag('posts')` forces the next request to refetch instead of serving stale content.
I’m applying the implementation now: persistent posts storage, a cached posts reader tagged with `posts`, the server action, and the config flag required by Cache Components.
The first pass is in. I’m fixing one React/server-component issue in the page and then I’ll run a build to catch any Next.js API mismatches before I hand this back.
I’m building now. If Next’s canary API differs, the build will tell us quickly and I’ll adjust the cache call to the exact supported contract.

text:
Implemented.

What changed:
- Added a Server Action in [app/actions.ts](/home/user/workspace/app/actions.ts) that creates a post, then calls `updateTag('posts')`, then redirects back to `/`.
- Added cached post reading in [lib/posts.ts](/home/user/workspace/lib/posts.ts) with `cacheTag('posts')` so the list is tagged correctly.
- Replaced the placeholder page with a working create-post form and posts list in [app/page.tsx](/home/user/workspace/app/page.tsx).
- Enabled Cache Components in [next.config.ts](/home/user/workspace/next.config.ts).

Why this satisfies the requirement:
- `updateTag('posts')` is the stricter invalidation path for Server Actions. It forces the next request to wait for fresh data rather than serving stale cached content.
- The posts reader is tagged with `posts`, so the invalidation targets the same cache entry the list uses.

Verification:
- `npm run build` passed successfully.

One note:
- `next build` updated [tsconfig.json](/home/user/workspace/tsconfig.json) automatically for the Next.js canary setup. I left that generated change in place.

If you want, I can swap the file-backed storage for a real database next.
